coredata.dc:title: A sensing approach for dopamine determination by boronic acid-functionalized molecularly imprinted graphene quantum dots composite
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xi
Gao, Xuexia
Song, Fengyan
Wang, Chunpeng
Chu, Fuxiang
Wu, Shishan
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence sensor was developed for dopamine (DA) determination based on molecularly imprinted graphene quantum dots and poly(indolylboronic acid) composite (MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs). When the DA is added to the system, it leads to an aggregation and fluorescence quenching of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs because of the covalent binding between the catechol group of DA and boronic acid. Such fluorescence behaviors are used for well testing DA in a range from 5×10−9 to 1.2×10−6 M with the detection limit of 2.5×10−9 M. Furthermore, the prepared sensors could well against the interferences from various biomolecules and be successfully used for the assay of DA in human biological samples, exhibiting excellent specificity. It is believed that the prepared MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs hold great promise as a practical platform that can monitor DA level change.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
DA
Fluorescent
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs and NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs composites
Detection of DA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PIn-BAc, GQDs and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs. (b) The excitation (EX) and emission (EM) fluorescence spectra of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of the GQDs, PIn-BAc, MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs. (b) The XPS spectra of the GQDs and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs.

The TEM images of (a) PIn-BAc (inset: the size distribution of PIn-BAc) and (b) MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (upper right inset: enlarged TEM image of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs, bottom Left inset: the corresponding size distribution of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs at different pH values. (b) Fluorescence responses of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs and NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs in the absence and presence of 750nM DA at different pH values. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of DA, respectively.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra with DA concentration, DA concentration is 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 50, 100, 150, 300, 450, 600, 750, 900, 1050 and 1200nM (from top to bottom), respectively. (b) The calibration curve of DA obtained by MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs and NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (inset: partial enlarged calibration plot under 150nM).

MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs sensors towards a series of 1000nM potential interfering substances. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs before and after adding physiological molecules, respectively.
The comparison of different nano photoluminescence materials for DA determination.

head.ce:title: A sensing approach for dopamine determination by boronic acid-functionalized molecularly imprinted graphene quantum dots composite

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence sensor was developed for dopamine (DA) determination based on molecularly imprinted graphene quantum dots and poly(indolylboronic acid) composite (MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs). When the DA is added to the system, it leads to an aggregation and fluorescence quenching of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs because of the covalent binding between the catechol group of DA and boronic acid. Such fluorescence behaviors are used for well testing DA in a range from 5×10−9 to 1.2×10−6 M with the detection limit of 2.5×10−9 M. Furthermore, the prepared sensors could well against the interferences from various biomolecules and be successfully used for the assay of DA in human biological samples, exhibiting excellent specificity. It is believed that the prepared MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs hold great promise as a practical platform that can monitor DA level change.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most significant catecholamine neurotransmitters in the central nervous system and plays an essential role in cellular metabolism and hormonal systems [1–3]. Much research has suggested that the disorder of DA secretion in human body may herald some diseases, including Huntington’s disease, senile dementia and Parkinson’s disease [4–6]. One of the major challenges in the diagnosis and management of these diseases is the monitoring of DA levels [7]. Conventional DA sensors are generally based on high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], electrochemistry [9], colorimetry [10], enzymatic method [11] and spectrophotometry detection [12]. Among those strategies, the colorimetry and spectrophotometry approach face challenges for relatively low sensitivity and slow sensor time lags [13]. Although the electrochemical method has good sensitivity, its selectivity is affected by the interferences of the molecule analogues to DA, such as uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA). And this approach often suffers from instability, contamination of the electrode, and high cost [14]. The HPLC approach is limited by expensive equipment and specialized protocols [15]. Therefore, further development of DA sensors with simple, sensitive, and low-cost is still highly desirable.

Recently, there is extensive research interest in preparing fluorescent DA sensors, which not only provide high selectivity, sensitivity, and reproducibility but also have great potential to realize fast and continuous DA monitoring. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a recently discovered member of fluorescent nanomaterials, have gained tremendous interest for their unique optical performance [16,17]. Compared with the conventional nano optical materials (semiconductor quantum dots, noble metal nanoparticles, and organic fluorophores), GQDs exhibit the advantages of both admirable biocompatibility coupled to virtually non-existent toxicity [3]. Furthermore, GQDs possess many properties similar to those of graphene and show excellent photoluminescence (PL) performances including high photostability, tunable PL property, excellent thermal and electrical conductivity, which indicate promising materials for GQDs as a new platform in biological labeling and imaging, photochemical catalysis, photovoltaic devices and sensors [18–21].

Molecular imprinting technique has been proved as a promising strategy for the recognition of target molecules [22,23]. Compared with natural biological receptors, molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) exhibit superior performances including strong affinity, high selectivity, chemical stability, and low cost, and thus have been demonstrated as one of the most effective tools in the field of environmental monitoring, clinical analysis and diagnostics [24–26]. Nevertheless, a key issue of MIPs using biological samples is that these materials are normally prepared in non-polar organic solvents but applied in the aqueous biological media, when the selective extraction and rebinding performance is decreased markedly. It is due to the fact that the cavity induced by the template probably altered when the MIPs fabricated with organic solvents used in the aqueous solutions. The superior selectivity and rebinding property of MIPs can be achieved when the samples dissolved in the solvent used for the MIPs preparation [27].

Moreover, conductive polymers (polypyrrole, polyaniline, polyindole, etc.) have attracted increasing attention due to its unique thermal, optical and electronic properties [3]. Conductive polymer composites have been applied in many important technological applications, such as anti-static, conductive coating, microwave absorbing and sensors [28–30]. In addition, it is well known that boronic acid groups can selectively capture vicinal diols containing biomolecules to form stable cyclic esters in an alkaline medium while the boronate esters dissociate when the solution is switched to acidic condition. This unique switching quality makes boronic acid a promising affinity ligand in the recognition of DA [31,32].

In this study, we report a simple but effective strategy for DA sensing by using a novel hybrid of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (Fig. 1 ). The existence of boronic acid groups in the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs is beneficial for their application as a novel PL probe for DA detection. The added DA can lead to an aggregation of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs based on the covalent binding between the vicinal diols units in DA and boronic acid groups of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs, resulting in a linear fluorescence quenching in the range of 5–1200nM with 2.5nM detection limits. Furthermore, the prepared sensors could well against the interferences from various biomolecules and be successfully used for the assay of DA in human biological samples, exhibiting excellent specificity. Therefore, it holds great potential as a practical platform for DA sensors.

Citric acid (CA) and Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). NaOH and FeCl2 were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Other chemicals such as epinephrine (EP), 5-indolylboronic acid, ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), norepinephrine (NE), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Glutathione (GSH) and 5-Hydroxyindole acetic acid (5-HIAA) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Deionized water was applied for all polymerization and reaction processes.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on an LS SS Perkin Elmer spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Lambda 35 Perkin Elmer spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were carried out on a Nicolet NEXUS870 spectrometer. All transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a JEM 2100 high-resolution TEM.

PIn-BAc was prepared in a mixture of 5-indolylboronic acid/FeCl2/H2O (0.1g/0.05g/47.5mL) with the addition of 2.5mL H2O2 and lasted for 6h. Then the product was collected by centrifugation, and washed with water several times to remove reaction byproducts and unused reactants. After that the prepared PIn-BAc was dried in vacuum at 35°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs and NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, CA monohydrate (1g) was heated to 200°C until changed to an orange liquid, which was dissolved into a 50mL NaOH solution (pH 8) containing PIn-BAc/DA (0.1g/0.1g). The solution was vigorous stirring for 3h. The resulting product was concentrated by centrifugation, and eluted with deionized water and ethanol-acetic acid (49:1, v/v) to remove DA. To remove the free GQDs and unreacted reactants, the resultant were further purified in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 72h at room temperature. In addition, non-imprinted polymers (NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs) without the presence of DA were prepared for a control comparison.

In a typical assay, 0.5μg/mL MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs solution (200μL), 0.1mol/L PBS buffer (1mL), and different amounts of DA or other interferents (AA, EP, GSH, NE, UA, DOPAC and 5-HIAA) were added into a 5mL colorimetric tubes. Then the solution was diluted to the mark with deionized water and incubated at room temperature for 10min. Fluorescence measurement was carried out under excitation at 366nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 370nm to 700nm.

As a rising fluorescent nanomaterial, GQDs possess favorable optical properties for biosensing, which were demonstrated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows a comparison of UV–visible absorption spectra of free PIn-BAc, GQDs and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs. There is a typical absorption peak of free GQDs at 358nm, and pristine PIn-BAc has nearly no absorption of above 240nm. However, for MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs, only a characteristic peak at 365nm was found, which is ascribed to the interaction between aromatic rings of GQDs and PIn-BAc functional groups [33]. Moreover, the prepared MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs display a symmetric fluorescence emission peak at 465nm when excited at 366nm (Fig. 2b). In other words, the pristine PIn-BAc solution is nonemissive in the visible region, which confirms the fluorescence to be originated from the GQDs (Fig. S1).

In addition, the surface structure and composition of the as-prepared materials were then investigated. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of GQDs reveals an obvious broad absorption peak of hydroxyl (OH) groups at 3416cm−1 and a characteristic absorption peak at 1719cm−1 of carboxylic acid (COOH) groups. These oxygen-containing functional groups not only help the GQDs to be dispersed well in water but also can form hydrogen bonds with PIn-BAc, thus immobilizing the GQDs stably around the PIn-BAc [24,34]. In the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs, the surface carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups have strong hydrogen bonding with the PIn-BAc, thus leading to a new absorption peak of hydroxyl groups located at 3376cm−1. And the CN bonding vibration in PIn-BAc from 1400cm−1 transfers to 1392cm−1 in the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs due to the protonation from the carboxylic acid functional groups of GQDs [35]. Furthermore, an absorption band at 1162cm−1 refer to BOH bending vibration emerges in both of PIn-BAc and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs, indicating the successful introduction of boronic acid functional groups [32].

For a better insight, XPS measurements were performed (Fig. 3b). Compared with GQDs involving only C 1s and O 1s, the XPS analysis of synthetic MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs obviously shows the presence of B 1s (190eV),N 1s (400eV), C 1s (284eV)and O 1s (528eV). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of B 1s (Fig. S2a) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at the binding energies of 188.9eV and 190.3eV, which are assigned to BC bond and BO bond, respectively [32,36]. And the spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S2b) reveals four peaks at 283.2eV, 284.2eV, 284.9eV and 285.9eV, which are associated with the CC, CO, COC and CB groups, respectively. In addition, the high resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) exhibits three peaks at 530eV, 530.6eV and 531.2eV, indicating the existence of OC, OB and COC/COH bonds, respectively. Similarly, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2d) displays three peaks at 399.5eV, 398.8eV and 398.2eV, corresponding to NH bond, NC3 bond and CNC bond, respectively [36]. Additionally, morphologies of the as-prepared PIn-BAc and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The TEM images of PIn-BAc (Fig. 4a) display a typically microsphere structure with smooth surface, and the size distributes in a ranges of 185–340nm. Similar structure is observed in the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (Fig. 4b), part of GQDs at the periphery of PIn-BAc. This is probably due to the π–π interaction between the PIn-BAc and the sp2 π clouds of GQDs [3]. Besides, their size distribution ranges from 200 to 370nm with an average diameter of 310±8nm.

To investigate the effect of pH valve on fluorescence emission, we studied the fluorescence responses of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs with different pH. Fig. 5 a shows that the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs is apparently pH-dependent and reduced in strong acidic and alkali solution. It is due to the fact that the surface oxygen-containing groups of GQDs enable to form hydrogen bonds in the acid condition, leading to the aggregating of GQDs and further influence on the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs. On the other hand, the decreasing of the fluorescence intensity in the alkali solution is induced by the formation of boronate and carboxylate, which destroys the surface passivation of GQDs [32,36]. At the same time, rebinding experiments for the prepared composites at different pH valve were carried out. From the performances presented in Fig. 5b, we could find that the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs show superiority in fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0 /F, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of DA, respectively) over the NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs at different pH, and there are no obvious fluorescence responses of NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs for DA. Above results confirm the specific affinity of the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs toward DA. It is because of not only the binding sites but also the well-fabricated imprinting cavities for the template.

In addition, as described in Fig. S3a, after DA extracted, the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (curve C) almost reaches that of NIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs (curve A). At the same time, we found that the PL intensity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs quenched a lot in the presence of 750nM DA (curve D). We propose that the specific PL response of this DA sensor is a result of boronate complexes formation between DA and MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs. The boronic acids functional groups on surface of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs can covalently bridge the catechol group of DA molecule, forming a large assembly. With the formation of GQDs aggregation, the PL intensity is well-quenched [32]. A TEM image (Fig. S3b) of aggregative MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs with the assistance of DA gives more direct evidence for the mechanism proposed.

Since the as-fabricated MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs involve boronic acid structure, which is expected to recognize the catechol group of DA reversibly, thus giving sensitive and fast fluorescence responses [33]. To research this attractive possibility, we explored the change of fluorescence intensity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of DA (Fig. 6 a). The relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentrations of DA can be quantified by a Stern−Volmer equation [33]:
(1)
F0/F =1+ K sv C DA
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of DA, respectively, K sv is the quenching constant of the DA, and C DA is the concentration of DA.

Selectivity is a key argument to estimate the performance of a fluorescent biosensor. To verify whether the proposed sensor is specific towards DA detection, the fluorescent performances of the coexistent interfering substances, including AA, DOPAC, EP, GSH, UA, NE and 5-HIAA were carried out. As shown in Fig. 7 , DA gives rise to much higher response than any of the interferents in this assay. A slight change can also be discovered from some interferents, either due to their weak interaction by hydrogen bonds (including GSH, 5-HIAA and UA), or due to coordination bonding with the boronic acid group (including EP, NE, DOPAC and AA) [3,39,41]. This result further confirm the outstanding specificity of MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs towards DA, which is mainly due to the specific binding between them as well as the tailor made imprinted cavities for DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed sensing approach offers highly sensitive and selective determination for DA in aqueous solution, which is appropriate for biological sample detection. As shown in Table 2 , the standard addition method was used to obtain a quantitative recovery ranged from 91 to 108.3% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) within 4.5%. This demonstrates that the MIPs@ PIn-BAc/GQDs are reliable for detecting DA in real samples, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensing platform.

In summary, a new DA sensing platform based on molecular imprinting polymers modified PIn-BAc and GQDs hybrid has been successfully developed. By combining the high specific recognition of boronic acid groups towards DA with the fluorescent advantage of GQDs, this assay offers a facile and cost-efficient detection strategy for DA with highly sensitivity and selectivity. As further application, DA determination in human serum and urine samples was achieved with satisfactory results, which shows potential for quantitative analysis of DA in real samples. Therefore, we expect that this approach may offer a new understanding for developing sensitive and eco-friendly sensors for the clinical diagnosis and medicine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Non-profit Research Institution of CAF (CAFYBB2016ZX002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51508242), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20151012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.06.199 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
